The most common tumor of the mediastinum appears to be related to statistical frequency across different articles. Specifically, several sources highlight the prevalence of lymphomas within the superior mediastinum, indicating that they constitute a significant number of diagnoses despite being diverse in nature (indolent vs. highly aggressive forms). Another key piece of information comes from the observation that thymomas account for approximately half of the tumors in the anterior region, making them notably prominent in this specific area of the mediastinum. Given these points, although lymphomas seem prevalent overall, the data specifically mentions thymomas as dominating the anterior segment where much activity centers around anterior mediastinal masses.

Lymphomas appear prominently throughout the evidence base, but detailed statistics indicate they are predominantly found in younger populations rather than broadly representing the majority of mediastinal tumors over all ages. Thymomas are consistently noted as comprising nearly half of all anterior mediastinal tumors and are highly referenced as a major component of this region. Therefore, given the emphasis on thymomas in the anterior mediastinum, they stand out as the most common tumor overall in this context.
